annotations.label: Basic algebraic operation

anchor.snippet: a basic algebraic operation is a mathematical operation similar to any one of the common operations of elementary algebra

annotations.note: Mathlib has the specific operations (+, ×, ^, etc.) but no general informal notion of 'basic algebraic operation'.

annotations.label: Algebraic operation on a set

anchor.snippet: may be defined more formally as a function that maps to

annotations.note: Mathlib models operations via typeclasses/functions but has no dedicated definition of an n-ary 'algebraic operation' as a function Sⁿ → S.

annotations.label: Arity and operand

anchor.snippet: The length of the tuples is called the arity of the operation

annotations.note: No Mathlib declaration captures the informal concepts of 'arity' and 'operand' of an operation.

annotations.label: Binary operation

anchor.snippet: the operation is called a binary operation and the operands form an ordered pair

annotations.note: Mathlib represents specific binary operations via Mul/Add typeclasses but has no generic 'BinaryOperation' definition.

annotations.label: Unary operation

anchor.snippet: A unary operation is an operation of arity one that has only one operand

annotations.note: No dedicated 'unary operation' definition exists; such maps are just functions α → α.

annotations.label: Ternary operation (triple product)

anchor.snippet: An example of a ternary operation (arity three) is the triple product

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the scalar triple product (triple_product_eq_det) as a specific ternary map, but not the general notion of a ternary operation.

annotations.label: Compound algebraic operation

anchor.snippet: The term algebraic operation may also be used for operations that may be defined by compounding basic algebraic operations

annotations.note: No Mathlib declaration formalizes the informal notion of compounding basic algebraic operations.

annotations.label: Coefficient

anchor.snippet: A coefficient is a numerical value, or letter representing a numerical constant, that multiplies a variable

annotations.note: Mathlib has Polynomial.coeff for coefficients of polynomials, which is related infrastructure but not the informal elementary-algebra notion.

annotations.label: Term

anchor.snippet: A term is an addend or a summand

annotations.note: Mathlib has no declaration formalizing the informal notion of a 'term' (addend/summand) of an expression.

annotations.label: Exponent of zero gives 1

anchor.snippet: When the exponent is zero, the result is always 1

annotations.note: pow_zero states a ^ 0 = 1, exactly matching the claim.

annotations.label: Variables for unknown values

anchor.snippet: Variables may represent numbers whose values are not yet known

annotations.note: This is an informal description of the use of variables, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Variables for general problems

anchor.snippet: Variables allow one to describe general problems

annotations.note: Informal motivation for using variables, with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Variables for relationships

anchor.snippet: Variables allow one to describe mathematical relationships between quantities that may vary

annotations.note: Informal description of variables expressing relationships, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Commutativity of addition

anchor.snippet: a basic property of addition is commutativity which states that the order of numbers being added together does not matter

annotations.note: add_comm states a + b = b + a in any additive commutative structure, exactly the stated property.

annotations.label: Simplify added terms

anchor.snippet: Added terms are simplified using coefficients

annotations.note: This describes a syntactic simplification heuristic rather than a stated theorem.

annotations.label: Simplify multiplied terms

anchor.snippet: Multiplied terms are simplified using exponents

annotations.note: This describes a simplification heuristic (collecting factors into exponents) rather than a single theorem.

annotations.label: Combine like terms

anchor.snippet: Like terms are added together

annotations.note: Combining like terms is an informal simplification procedure with no dedicated Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Distributive property (multiplying out brackets)

anchor.snippet: Brackets can be "multiplied out", using the distributive property

annotations.note: left_distrib states a * (b + c) = a * b + a * c, exactly the distributive law used to expand brackets.

annotations.label: Factoring expressions

anchor.snippet: Expressions can be factored

annotations.note: Factoring is described as a manipulation procedure, not formalized as a single statement.

annotations.label: Equation

anchor.snippet: An equation states that two expressions are equal using the symbol for equality

annotations.note: The underlying equality relation is Lean's core Eq, but Mathlib has no declaration defining 'equation' as such.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: describes Pythagoras' law relating the length of the sides of a right angle triangle

annotations.note: Mathlib's Pythagorean theorem states dist² = dist² + dist² iff the angle is π/2, generalizing a²+b²=c² to Euclidean spaces.

annotations.label: Identity

anchor.snippet: such equations are called identities

annotations.note: An identity is an equation true for all values; Mathlib expresses these as universally quantified equalities but has no 'identity' definition.

annotations.label: Conditional equation

anchor.snippet: Conditional equations are true for only some values of the involved variables

annotations.note: No Mathlib declaration formalizes the informal notion of a 'conditional equation'.

annotations.label: Inequality

anchor.snippet: Inequalities are used to show that one side of the equation is greater, or less, than the other

annotations.note: The order relations < and ≤ (LT.lt/LE.le) provide the underlying infrastructure, but there is no 'inequality' definition itself.

annotations.label: Flipping inequality on negative multiplication

anchor.snippet: when multiplying or dividing by a negative number, the inequality symbol must be flipped

annotations.note: mul_lt_mul_of_neg_left (and _right) state that multiplying by a negative reverses a strict inequality.

annotations.label: Equality is an equivalence relation

anchor.snippet: equality is an equivalence relation , meaning it is reflexive

annotations.note: eq_equivalence proves Equivalence (@Eq α), i.e. equality is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.

annotations.label: Substitution property of equality

anchor.snippet: if two symbols are used for equal things, then one symbol can be substituted for the other in any true statement about the first

annotations.note: Lean's core Eq.subst (the Eq eliminator) is exactly the Leibniz substitution principle for equal terms.

annotations.label: Transitivity of inequalities

anchor.snippet: have the property of transitivity

annotations.note: lt_trans (and le_trans, aliased in Mathlib.Order.Basic) give transitivity of strict and non-strict inequalities.

annotations.label: Reversing an inequation

anchor.snippet: By reversing the inequation

annotations.note: ge_iff_le states a ≥ b ↔ b ≤ a, formalizing the swap of ≤ and ≥ by reversing an inequation.

annotations.label: Substitution

anchor.snippet: Substitution is replacing the terms in an expression to create a new expression

annotations.note: Substituting values into expressions is realized via function/polynomial evaluation but has no dedicated 'substitution' definition.

annotations.label: Zero-product property

anchor.snippet: then xy = 0 implies x = 0 or y = 0

annotations.note: mul_eq_zero states a * b = 0 ↔ a = 0 ∨ b = 0 in a domain, exactly the zero-product property.

annotations.label: Linear equation

anchor.snippet: Linear equations are so-called, because when they are plotted, they describe a straight line

annotations.note: Mathlib has LinearMap/AffineMap and lines, but no definition of 'linear equation' as an elementary-algebra object.

annotations.label: Child age problem

anchor.snippet: If you double the age of a child and add 4, the resulting answer is 12

annotations.note: This is a worked word problem, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: General solution of a one-variable linear equation

anchor.snippet: The general form of a linear equation with one variable, can be written as

annotations.note: No Mathlib theorem states the general solution x = -b/a of ax + b = 0, though field arithmetic lemmas could derive it.

annotations.label: Linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions

anchor.snippet: A linear equation with two variables has many (i.e. an infinite number of) solutions

annotations.note: No dedicated Mathlib statement asserts that a single linear equation in two variables has infinitely many solutions.

annotations.label: Father and son age problem

anchor.snippet: A father is 22 years older than his son

annotations.note: This is a worked word problem, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Quadratic equation

anchor.snippet: A quadratic equation is one which includes a term with an exponent of 2

annotations.note: Mathlib reasons about quadratics via discrim and related lemmas (a*(x*x)+b*x+c) but has no standalone 'quadratic equation' definition.

annotations.label: Standard form of a quadratic equation

anchor.snippet: we may divide by a and rearrange the equation into the standard form

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement formalizes normalizing a quadratic to monic standard form by dividing through by a.

annotations.label: Quadratic formula

anchor.snippet: leads to the quadratic formula

annotations.note: quadratic_eq_zero_iff gives the roots (-b ± s)/(2a) where s² = discrim, i.e. the quadratic formula.

annotations.label: Solving quadratics by factorization

anchor.snippet: Quadratic equations can also be solved using factorization

annotations.note: The factorization method relies on the zero-product property (mul_eq_zero), but the procedure itself is not a single formalized theorem.

annotations.label: Zero-product property for quadratics

anchor.snippet: It follows from the zero-product property that either

annotations.note: This is a direct application of mul_eq_zero to a factored quadratic (x-r)(x-s) = 0.

annotations.label: Quadratic with no real solution

anchor.snippet: has no real number solution since no real number squared equals −1

annotations.note: quadratic_ne_zero_of_discrim_ne_sq gives nonexistence of roots when the discriminant is not a square, covering x²+1=0 over ℝ, but the specific example is not a named result.

annotations.label: Root of multiplicity 2

anchor.snippet: Sometimes a quadratic equation has a root of multiplicity 2

annotations.note: quadratic_eq_zero_iff_of_discrim_eq_zero shows a unique (double) root -b/(2a) when the discriminant is zero, but 'multiplicity 2' per se uses Polynomial.rootMultiplicity.

annotations.label: Quadratics have two complex solutions

anchor.snippet: All quadratic equations have exactly two solutions in complex numbers

annotations.note: exists_quadratic_eq_zero gives existence of roots when the discriminant is a square (always true over the algebraically closed ℂ), but the exact 'two solutions counted with multiplicity' statement is not stated for quadratics specifically.

annotations.label: Complex solutions of a quadratic

anchor.snippet: Complex numbers first arise in the teaching of quadratic equations and the quadratic formula

annotations.note: Solving quadratics over ℂ is supported via exists_quadratic_eq_zero plus ℂ being algebraically closed, but this illustrative example is not itself formalized.

annotations.label: Exponential equation

anchor.snippet: An exponential equation is one which has the form

annotations.note: Mathlib has rpow/exp but no definition of 'exponential equation' as an elementary-algebra object.

annotations.label: Logarithmic equation

anchor.snippet: A logarithmic equation is an equation of the form

annotations.note: Mathlib has Real.log but no definition of a 'logarithmic equation'.

annotations.label: Radical equation

anchor.snippet: A radical equation is one that includes a radical sign, which includes square roots

annotations.note: Mathlib has roots/Real.sqrt but no definition of a 'radical equation'.

annotations.label: nth root as exponential form

anchor.snippet: Recall that an n th root can be rewritten in exponential format

annotations.note: Real.rpow_inv_natCast_pow states (x^(1/n))^n = x for x ≥ 0, formalizing the nth root as the exponent 1/n.

annotations.label: Elimination method

anchor.snippet: An example of solving a system of linear equations is by using the elimination method

annotations.note: The elimination solving procedure is illustrated by example and is not formalized as a stated theorem.

annotations.label: Substitution method

anchor.snippet: Another way of solving the same system of linear equations is by substitution

annotations.note: The substitution solving procedure is illustrated by example and is not formalized as a stated theorem.

annotations.label: Inconsistent system

anchor.snippet: Such a system is called inconsistent

annotations.note: Mathlib reasons about solvability of linear systems but has no definition of an 'inconsistent system'.

annotations.label: System with infinitely many solutions

anchor.snippet: There are also systems which have infinitely many solutions

annotations.note: No dedicated Mathlib statement formalizes this illustrative example of a system with infinitely many solutions.

annotations.label: Underdetermined system

anchor.snippet: Systems with more variables than the number of linear equations are called underdetermined

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition for 'underdetermined system' of linear equations.

annotations.label: Overdetermined system

anchor.snippet: A system with a higher number of equations than variables is called overdetermined

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition for 'overdetermined system' of linear equations.
